name: Sodium (Na+)
description: Sodium is the major extracellular cation. It plays a central role in maintaining normal water distribution, osmotic pressure, and acid-base balance in the extracellular fluid.
interpretation: Hyponatremia (<135 mEq/L) can result from excess water retention (e.g., heart failure, SIADH) or sodium loss (e.g., diuretics, vomiting). Hypernatremia (>145 mEq/L) typically indicates a water deficit relative to sodium, often due to inadequate water intake or excessive fluid loss (e.g., diabetes insipidus).
warnings: Pseudohyponatremia can occur in the setting of severe hyperlipidemia or hyperproteinemia.
Hyperglycemia causes a dilutional hyponatremia; correct sodium by adding 1.6 mEq/L for every 100 mg/dL increase in glucose above normal.
references: 1. Adrogué HJ, Madias NE. Hyponatremia. N Engl J Med. 2000;342(21):1581-1589.
2. Adrogué HJ, Madias NE. Hypernatremia. N Engl J Med. 2000;342(20):1493-1499.
examples: A patient with a serum sodium of 120 mEq/L presents with confusion and requires careful evaluation of volume status.
notes: Serum samples are preferred. Avoid hemolysis.
